does: This paper introduces the Rational Sparse Autoencoder (RSAE), which replaces the fixed nonlinear activations in standard sparse autoencoders with trainable rational functions. The core contribution is demonstrating that rational activations—ratios of polynomials—can uniformly approximate the activation primitives used by existing SAE families like ReLU, JumpReLU, and TopK while providing greater flexibility to adapt to the actual pre-activation geometry of learned representations. The method is realized through a two-stage pipeline: initialization that transfers pre-trained SAE weights, computes rational coefficients using relaxed Remez exchange on synthetic data, and calibrates scale parameters; followed by fine-tuning on real data. This approach decouples the sparsity mechanism from hard-coded architectural choices, allowing the model to discover a better reconstruction-sparsity trade-off.
connects: This work builds on the established mechanistic interpretability framework that uses sparse autoencoders to decompose neural network activations into interpretable features. It directly responds to limitations of current SAE families—which fix their encoder nonlinearity upfront, potentially misaligning with the actual distribution and geometry of pre-activations in the models being interpreted. The paper extends prior research on flexible activation functions and rational approximation theory by applying them to the interpretability domain. It relates to broader efforts in SAE design that have progressively refined the sparsity-reconstruction trade-off through architectural innovations like JumpReLU and TopK variants, while engaging with classical approximation theory to provide theoretical justification for why rational functions can encompass existing approaches.
enables: This work opens new directions for improving mechanistic interpretability by enabling more adaptive and task-specific SAE designs. The framework of learnable rational activations could extend beyond sparse autoencoders to other interpretability methods that rely on fixed nonlinearities, and could facilitate better extraction of features from larger or more complex models. It suggests future work on optimizing the reconstruction-sparsity Pareto frontier through learned activation families, potentially improving the fidelity of feature dictionaries used in mechanistic analysis. The approach also points toward hybrid interpretability-performance methods where rational functions optimize for interpretability objectives while maintaining computational efficiency, and could inspire broader exploration of how flexible function classes adapt to high-dimensional learned representations across different model architectures and domains.
